coredata.dc:title: Co-transport and remobilization of Cu and Pb in quartz column by carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Kamrani, Salahaddin
Rezaei, Mohsen
Kord, Mehdi
Baalousha, Mohammed
coredata.dc:description: Carbon nanoparticles such graphene, carbon nanotubes, and carbon dots offer the potential to improve environmental treatment technologies due to their unique properties such as low toxicity and high metal sorption capacity. However, there are no studies on facilitated transport and remobilization of pre-sorbed metals by carbon dot (CD) nanoparticles in quartz sand columns. Here, we investigate the effects of solution ionic strength (IS; 1, 100 and 200 mM NaCl) and pH (Chen et al., 2017; Chen et al., 2010; Cornell and Schwertmann, 2006), initial CD concentration (200, 400, 600 and 800 mg L−1), and clay content (10, 20 and 30%w kaolinite) in quartz sand columns on the transport, retention and remobilization of Cu and Pb in saturated (upward flow) quartz porous media. Batch sorption experiments were employed to underpin the findings of the column transport experiments. Both CD and quartz adsorbed Cu and Pb from water, but adsorption was higher on CD than quartz. Co-transport experiment demonstrated the CD-facilitated transport of Cu and Pb. Sequential transport experiments (first three phases) demonstrated the retention of Cu and Pb in the quartz column, with higher retention of Pb compared to Cu. The Cu and Pb retention was attributed to their sorption on the quartz grains and precipitation under the experimental conditions investigated in this study. Cu retention increased with the increase in ionic strength, pH and clay content. Pb was nearly totally retained in the quartz column at all experimental conditions. The subsequent injection of CD resulted in Cu and Pb remobilization to different extents, except in the presence of high kaolinite concentration. CD is most efficient in remobilizing Cu and Pb at 400 mg L−1 CD concentration and under low ionic strength (ca. 1–100 mM), low pH (ca. 6) and in the absence of clays. Deviation from these conditions results in reduced remobilization of Cu and Pb.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dot
Quartz sand column
Kaolinite
Cu and Pb remobilization
Facilitated transport

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst misctext primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Pb and Cu sorption on CDs
Effect of CD concentration on Cu and Pb adsorption on CDs and quartz
Effect of pH and ionic strength on Cu and Pb adsorption on CDs and quartz
Effect of experimental conditions on facilitated transport and remobilization of quartz pre-sorbed Cu and Pb
Effect of ionic strength and pH on cotransport of CDs, Cu and Pb
Effect of CD concentration on the sequential transport of CD, Cu and Pb
Effect of pH on the sequential transport of CD, Cu and Pb
Effect of ionic strength on the sequential transport of CD, Cu and Pb
Effect of clay content on the sequential transport of CD, Cu and Pb

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Batch experiments of CD, Cu, Pb under different CD concentration: (A: Cu (II) adsorption and B; Pb (II) adsorption). All experiments were performed in 10 g quartz sand, 25 mL Deionized water, C0 (CD): 50 to 800 mg·l−1, C0 (Cu): 10 mg·l−1, C0 (Pb): 10 mg·l−1, pH 6 and IS 1 mM NaCl and equilibrium time 120 min background solution contain: NaCl (1 mM), NaHCO3 (1 mM).

Batch experiments of CD, Cu, Pb under different pH: (A: Cu (II) adsorption and B; Pb (II) adsorption). All experiments were performed in average 10 g quartz sand, 25 mL Deionized water, C0 (CD): 400 mg·l−1, C0 (Cu): 10 mg·l−1, C0 (Pb): 10 mg·l−1, IS 1 mM NaCl and equilibrium time 120 min background solution contain: NaCl (1 mM), NaHCO3 (1 mM).

Batch experiments of CD, Cu, Pb under different IS solution: (A: Cu (II) adsorption and B; Pb (II) adsorption). All experiments were performed in average 10 g quartz sand, 25 mL Deionized water, C0 (CD): 400 mg·l−1, C0 (Cu): 10 mg·l−1, C0 (Pb): 10 mg·l−1, pH 6 and equilibrium time 120 min background solution contain: NaCl (1 mM), NaHCO3 (1 mM).

Cotransport breakthrough curves (CBTCs) under different conditions (A: pH 6 and 1 mM NaCl, B: pH 6 and 100 mM NaCl, C: pH 8 and 1 mM NaCl, D: % mass recovered in the effluent). All experiments were performed in C0 (CD): 400 mg·l−1, C0 (Cu): 10 mg·l−1, C0 (Pb): 10 mg·l−1, background solution contain: NaCl (1 mM), NaHCO3 (1 mM), 2 rpm (6.15 mL·min−1) flow rate.
CBTCs: 1. Background flushing 2. Background with Cu and Pb and CD 3. Background flushing.

Sequential breakthrough curves (SBTCs) of CD, Cu, Pb and Tracer under different CD concentrations (A: 200, B: 400, C: 600 and D: 800 mg·l−1, E: % mass recovered in the effluent). All experiments were performed in C0 (CD): 400 mg·l−1, C0 (Cu): 10 mg·l−1, C0 (Pb): 10 mg·l−1, pH for all solution 6, background solution contain: NaCl (1 mM), NaHCO3 (1 mM), 2 rpm (6.15 mL·min−1) flow rate.
1. Background flushing 2. Background with Cu and Pb 3. Background flushing 4. Background with CD 5. Background flushing.

Sequential breakthrough curves (SBTCs) of CD, Cu, Pb and Tracer under different pH (A: pH 6, B: pH 7, C: pH 8, D: % mass recovered in the effluent). All experiments were performed in C0 (CD): 400 mg·l−1, C0 (Cu): 10 mg·l−1, C0 (Pb): 10 mg·l−1, background solution contain: NaCl (1 mM), NaHCO3 (1 mM), 2 rpm (6.15 mL·min−1) flow rate. SBTCs: 1. Background flushing 2. Background with Cu and Pb 3. Background flushing 4. Background with CD 5. Background flushing.

Sequential breakthrough curves (SBTCs) of CD, Cu, Pb and Tracer under different IS (A:1 mM NaCl, B:100 Mm NaCl, C:200 Mm NaCl, D: % mass recovered in the effluent). All experiments were performed in C0 (CD): 400 mg·l−1, C0 (Cu): 10 mg·l−1, C0 (Pb): 10 mg·l−1, pH for all solution 6, background solution contain: NaCl (1 mM), NaHCO3 (1 mM), 2 rpm (6.15 mL·min−1) flow rate. SBTCs: 1. Background flushing 2. Background with Cu and Pb 3. Background flushing 4. Background with CD 5. Background flushing.

Sequential breakthrough curves (SBTCs) of CD, Cu, Pb and Tracer under different porous media (A: 0% kaolin, B: 10% kaolin, C: 20% kaolin and D: 30% kaolin, E: % mass recovered in the effluent). All experiments were performed in C0 (CD): 400 mg·l−1, C0 (Cu): 10 mg·l−1, C0 (Pb): 10 mg·l−1, pH for all solution 6, background solution contain: NaCl (1 mM), NaHCO3 (1 mM), 2 rpm (6.15 mL·min−1) flow rate.
1. Background flushing 2. Background with Cu and Pb 3. Background flushing 4. Background with CD 5. Background flushing.
Characteristics of sequential and cotransport columns experiment. Cu and Pb are constant concentrations (10 mg·l−1) in the experiments.
Characteristics of batch experiment. Cu and Pb are constant concentrations (10 mg·l−1).
Zeta potential for CDs and sand grains at different pH and IS solutions.

head.ce:title: Co-transport and remobilization of Cu and Pb in quartz column by carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Applied Geology, Faculty of Earth Sciences, Hydrogeology, Kharazmi University, Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Earth Sciences, Faculty of Sciences, University of Kurdistan, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center for Environmental Nanoscience and Risk, Arnold School of Public Health, University of South Carolina, USA

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Center for Environmental Nanoscience and Risk

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Environmental Health Sciences, Arnold School of Public Health, University of South Carolina, USA

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: M. Baalousha, Center for Environmental Nanoscience and Risk, Arnold School of Public Health, University of South Carolina, USA.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Center for Environmental Nanoscience and Risk

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon nanoparticles such graphene, carbon nanotubes, and carbon dots offer the potential to improve environmental treatment technologies due to their unique properties such as low toxicity and high metal sorption capacity. However, there are no studies on facilitated transport and remobilization of pre-sorbed metals by carbon dot (CD) nanoparticles in quartz sand columns. Here, we investigate the effects of solution ionic strength (IS; 1, 100 and 200 mM NaCl) and pH (Chen et al., 2017; Chen et al., 2010; Cornell and Schwertmann, 2006), initial CD concentration (200, 400, 600 and 800 mg L−1), and clay content (10, 20 and 30%w kaolinite) in quartz sand columns on the transport, retention and remobilization of Cu and Pb in saturated (upward flow) quartz porous media. Batch sorption experiments were employed to underpin the findings of the column transport experiments. Both CD and quartz adsorbed Cu and Pb from water, but adsorption was higher on CD than quartz.

Co-transport experiment demonstrated the CD-facilitated transport of Cu and Pb. Sequential transport experiments (first three phases) demonstrated the retention of Cu and Pb in the quartz column, with higher retention of Pb compared to Cu. The Cu and Pb retention was attributed to their sorption on the quartz grains and precipitation under the experimental conditions investigated in this study. Cu retention increased with the increase in ionic strength, pH and clay content. Pb was nearly totally retained in the quartz column at all experimental conditions. The subsequent injection of CD resulted in Cu and Pb remobilization to different extents, except in the presence of high kaolinite concentration. CD is most efficient in remobilizing Cu and Pb at 400 mg L−1 CD concentration and under low ionic strength (ca. 1–100 mM), low pH (ca. 6) and in the absence of clays. Deviation from these conditions results in reduced remobilization of Cu and Pb.

ce:section.ce:para: Understanding the factors affecting transport and fate of heavy metals such as copper and lead in porous media is essential for processes such as landfill leachate filtration, land application of sewage sludge and groundwater recharge (Lim et al., 2015). Metals such as copper and lead may show strong affinity toward the porous media and significantly lower the aqueous phase concentration, and thus may result in metal retention in the porous media (Akbour et al., 2002). Additionally, metals may undergo precipitation and formation of large particles under certain photochemical conditions, which may result in their retention in the porous media by straining mechanisms (Chen et al., 2017).

Engineered nanoparticles may have high affinity to metals due to their high surface area and functional groups content (Louie and Pettibone, 2016). Additionally, engineered nanoparticles may be highly mobile in porous media due to their small size and high colloidal stability under favorable conditions due to their high surface charge (Zhuang et al., 2003). These attributes of engineered nanoparticles enable them to act as carriers for metals, and thus facilitate their transport with the groundwater flow (Hu et al., 2010; Yin et al., 2010). Numerous studies demonstrated colloid-facilitated transport of metals such as Cu2+, Cd2+, Ni2+ and Pb2+ in the presence of natural (kaolinite; Sen et al., 2002; Sun et al., 2010, Bactria; Pang and Simunek, 2006) and engineered nanoparticles (titanium dioxide (TiO2); Fang et al., 2011, hydroxyapatite nanoparticles(nHAP); Wang et al., 2011). However, water chemistry such as high ionic strength may result in nanoparticle aggregation and retention in the water column by attachment to the porous media grains or by straining mechanisms (Sharma et al., 2014). For example, Wang et al. (2011) reported the ionic composition and ionic strength effects on the facilitated transport of Cu2+ with nHAP, where the amount of nHAP attached to the sand column increased with increasing ionic strength. The high nanoparticle mobility and metal affinity can result in remobilization of pre-sorbed metals in contaminated sites (Zhou et al., 2016). For instance, carbon based ENPs (such as fullerene, carbon nanotubes, carbon nano-onions, and graphene oxide) have been investigated for the removal from contaminated porous media (Mauter and Elimelech, 2008; Seymour et al., 2012).

Carbon dots (CDs), a new member of the carbon based engineered nanoparticles is widely used in various industries. CDs have important properties such as high surface area, and high metal affinity and sorption capacity due to the abundance of surface functional groups such as carboxylic, phenolic and hydroxyl groups (Wang and Hu, 2014). Although there are many studies investigating the interactions between engineered nanoparticles and metals, co-transport of CDs and metals such as copper and lead, and mobilization of pre-sorbed metals using CDs have not been investigated yet. Furthermore, there is currently a lack of understanding of how environmental factors such as pH, ionic strength, and clay content affect CD-facilitate metal transport and mobilization.

Therefore, this study aims to evaluate the influence of environmental physicochemical conditions (e.g., ionic strength, pH and initial CD concentration) and porous media properties (e.g., different %w.t clay content) on CD, Pb, and Cu transport and retention in quartz sand column, and the role of CD on Pb and Cu transport and mobilization in soils.

Stock solutions of 10 mg L−1 Pb (II) and 10 mg L−1 Cu (II) were prepared from Pb(Cl)2 and Cu(Cl)2 (Merck, Germany) salts. Citric acid (C6H8O7 - Merck, Germany) was used for CD synthesis. NaCl and NaHCO3 (Merck, Germany) were used for background solution and ionic strength adjustment. Solution pH Adjustments were made prior to experiments using 0.01 M HCl and 0.01 M KOH.

CDs were synthesized from citric acid using a simple, one-step polymerization reaction method according to the method reported elsewhere (Kamrani et al., 2017). Briefly, 10 mg of citric acid was mixed with 10 ml distilled water in 100 ml volumetric flask and followed by polymerization at 250 °C for 60 min. The resulting suspension was diluted with 20 ml deionized water and centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 45 min to remove large particles produced during the synthesis procedure. To remove the ultrafine particles (<1 nm), CDs were dialyzed against millipore water using a dialysis membrane (MWCO of 3 kD) for 45 min by the centrifuge at 6000 rpm.

Electrophoretic mobility and z-average hydrodynamic diameter of CDs were measured at 25 °C under different pH and IS conditions using laser doppler electrophoresis and dynamic light scattering (DLS), respectively (Zetaplus - Brookhaven-USA). Zeta potential was calculated from the measured electrophoretic mobility using Henry's function and Smoluchowski approximation (Baalousha et al., 2012). Pb and Cu effluent concentrations were measured by Atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS, Varian AA240 FS, USA).

Quartz (with ∼99.38% SiO2 and 0.27 Fe2O3 based on XRF analysis) was obtained from the Top Quartzite Formation (north of Karaj, Iran). The quartzite was crushed with a jaw crusher and then sieved. Quartz grains had a size between 0.50 and 1 mm, and the approximate average grain diameter was 0.6 mm. Sand was cleaned following the protocol by Torkzaban et al. (2010). The electrophoretic mobility of quartz sand grains at different ionic strength and pH conditions were determined using Zeta sizer (BI-EKA, Brookhaven, Holtsville, NY).

Kaolinite was obtained from Karaj plain and sieved. Kaolinite was prepared following the protocol by Arroyo et al. (2005). To evaluate the effect of clay content on CD, Cu and Pb transport, sand column was amended with different wt% clay (Kaolinite). The surface potential of kaolinite at pH 6 was −33 mV as reported by Yukselen and Kaya (2003).

Treated sand was wet-packed into an acrylic organic glass column (25 cm length × 4 cm inner diameter) with stainless mesh screens on both ends. Each column contained approximately 500 ± 10 g of sand and had an average bed porosity of 0.38–0.42 ± 0.01. Water was introduced to the column using a peristaltic pump (Shenchen pump, BT100K, China) in the upward direction at a constant Darcy's velocity of 0.49 cm min−1 (2 rpm) which is a representative value for groundwater flow (Chen et al., 2010).

Two types of column experiments were performed: 1) sequential elution experiments which was the focus of this research and 2) cotransport column experiments. The total duration of each cotransport and sequential elution experiments were approximately 200 and 260 min, respectively. One pore volume for quartz and quartz-clay columns were 18 and 22 min respectively.

Sequential transport experiments were used to evaluate the CD ability to remobilize presorbed Cu and Pb in quartz column and experiments were conducted in 5 steps. Step1: the packed column was first flushed with 3 pore volumes (PV) of the background electrolyte solution (1 mM NaHCO3 + 1 mM NaCl) to equilibrate the chemical conditions and to establish steady state flow. Step 2: the background solution containing 10 mg·l−1 PbCl2 and 10 mg·l−1 CuCl2 was injected at a constant flow rate for 2 PVs to allow Cu and Pb to sorb on the quartz surfaces. Step 3: the background solution was fed to the column for 3 PVs to wash weakly retained Cu and Pb in the quartz column. Step 4: the background solution containing different concentration of CD (200, 400, 600 and 800 mg·l−1) was injected for 2 PVs to evaluate the CD ability to remobilize Cu and Pb sorbed on quartz, and step 5: the column was flushed with 5 PVs until CDs were not detected in the effluent.

Cotransport experiments were used to evaluate the CD-facilitated transport of Cu and Pb and were conducted in 3 steps. Step1: 5 PVs background solution were injected to the column to establish steady state water flow conditions. Step 2: the background solution containing 10 mg·l−1 PbCl2, 10 mg·l−1 CuCl2 and 400 mg·l−1 CD was fed to the column at a constant flow rate for 2 PVs to evaluate the CD-facilitated transport of Cu and Pb, and step 3: the column was flushed with 5 PVs background solution to flush CD, Cu and Pb in the columns.

During the transport experiments, column effluent samples at desired time intervals (5 min) were collected in centrifuge tubes in 15 mL tubes. The collected sample was divided into three aliquots for measuring the CD, Cu and Pb concentrations. The area under the breakthrough curves was integrated to calculate CD, Cu and Pb concentrations in the effluent.

Column experiments were performed by varying one experimental condition at a time (e.g. pH, ionic strength, CD concentration, kaolinite content in the column), while maintaining all other parameters constant (Table 1 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Pb and Cu sorption on CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Cu and Pb sorption on CDs in the presence and absence of quartz and on quartz grains were performed by mixing CD and quartz sand (separately and together) with 20 ml electrolyte solution containing 10 mg L−1 Pb(Cl)2 and 10 mg L−1 Cu(Cl)2 solution for 120 min (Table 2 ). Cu and Pb sorption on CDs were examined using different CD concentrations (50, 100, 200, 400, 600 and 800 mg·l−1) at pH 6, and 1 mM IS. To investigate the pH and IS effects on metal sorption on CDs, experiments were performed at 400 mg L−1 CDs, different IS (1, 100 and 200 mM NaCl) and pH (Chen et al., 2017; Chen et al., 2010; Cornell and Schwertmann, 2006).

In the experiment with quartz, quartz particles were first separated with 0.1 mm stainless mesh screens and thus the remaining solution contained only CDs, Cu and Pb. Cu and Pb sorbed on CDs were separated from Cu and Pb ions by ultrafiltration at 3 kDa (3 kDa ≈ 1 nm) using centrifugal ultrafiltration units at 6000 rpm for 45 min. Quartz-sorbed metals were released by acid leaching by mixing quartz particles with 25 mL of 0.5% HCl, and the mixture was rotated end-over-end for 20 min. The concentration of Cu and Pb released from quartz surfaces by HCl were measured by AAS.

CD mobility within the saturated sand columns is determined by the interaction energy between the CDs and the surface of collectors (sand particles). To this end, DLVO (Derjaguin-Landau-Verwey-Overbeek) theory was applied to qualitatively explain the CD-CD and CD-quartz sand interactions (Tian et al., 2010). Based on DLVO theory, the total interaction potential between particles is the sum of attractive vdW potential and repulsive EDL potential. The interaction energy between CDs and between CDs and sand particles were calculated as explained by Trefalt et al. (2014); Kamrani et al. (2017).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of CD concentration on Cu and Pb adsorption on CDs and quartz
ce:section.ce:para: In the absence of quartz, Cu sorption on CDs increased from 53.7 to 79.1% and Pb sorption on CDs increased from 45.1 to 85.2% with the increased CD concentration from 50 to 400 mg L−1 CD and reach plateaued from 400 to 800 mg L−1 (Fig. 1 ). In the presence of quartz, Cu sorption on CDs increased from 12.05 to 55.2% and Pb sorption on CDs increased from 22.4 to 62.3% with the increase in CD concentration from 50 to 400 mg L−1 and reached plateaued from 400 to 800 mg. Cu and Pb sorption on CDs was lower by approximately 25% and 33% respectively in the presence of quartz. The reduced sorption of Cu and Pb on CDs in the presence of quartz can be attributed to competition of some sorption sites on the quartz surface with those on the CD surfaces. Approximately, 20% of Cu and Pb sorbed on the surface of quartz at 50 mg L−1 CD concentration, which decreased to 17% for Cu and 10% for Pb at 800 mg L−1 CD (Fig. 1). The acid-washed quartz sand used in this study contains a small proportion of iron oxyhydroxides (0.27% Fe2O3 based XRF analysis), which can act as active sites for sorption of CDs (Kamrani et al., 2017; Tamez et al., 2016).

The low sorption of Cu and Pb to quartz (<20% at all CD concentrations) compared to their sorption on CDs (>50% at 400–800 mg L−1 CD, (Fig. 1) indicates the higher affinity of Cu and Pb to CDs than to quartz. The strong affinity of CDs to Cu and Pb can be attributed to the large CD specific surface area (5.23 × 102 m2 g−1), and active adsorption sites on CD; that is the large amount of oxygen-containing functional groups (carboxyl, hydroxyl group) on CD surfaces. These attributes suggest that CDs can facilitate transport, and potentially can remobilize pre-sorbed Cu and Pb in porous media. The efficient uptake of Cu and Pb on CDs is consistent with the results reported for other type of carbon nanomaterials such as graphene oxides (Hur et al., 2015; Ding et al., 2014; Zhou et al., 2016).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH and ionic strength on Cu and Pb adsorption on CDs and quartz
ce:section.ce:para: Sorption of Cu and Pb on CDs is higher than their sorption to quartz at all pHs (Fig. 2 ), indicating higher affinity of Cu and Pb to CDs than to quartz. The sorption of Pb (54–57%) to CDs was higher than sorption of Cu (41–44%) to CDs at all pHs, indicating higher affinity of Pb than Cu to CDs. The sorption of Cu to quartz was slightly lower (by approximately 2%) in the presence of CDs (Fig. 2a). The sorption of Pb to quartz was significantly lower (9, 13.5 and 14.2% at pH 6, 7 and 8, respectively) in the presence of CDs (Fig. 2b), which can be attributed to the high affinity of Pb to CDs (Shtepliuk et al., 2017). These results suggest that CD is likely to be more efficient in remobilizing pre-sorbed Pb on quartz at higher pH values.

Cu and Pb sorption on CD surfaces decreased with the increase in ionic strength (Fig. 3 ). This decrease in Cu and Pb sorption on CD surfaces can be attributed to 1) competition with Na for adsorption sites on CD surfaces 2) formation of soluble copper and lead chloride species at high chloride concentrations, and 3) decreased CD surface area due to increased CD aggregation at high ionic strengths (Table 3). Similarly, Cu and Pb adsorption on quartz grains decreased with the increase in ionic strength from 1 to 200 mM (Fig. 3) likely due to competition with Na for adsorption sites on quartz surfaces or formulation of copper and lead chloride species. At 1 mM NaCl, sorption of Cu and Pb on CDs is much higher than their sorption on quartz. However, at 200 mM NaCl, sorption of Cu and Pb on CD and quartz surfaces is nearly equal. These observations suggest that CD are likely to be more efficient in facilitating the transport and remobilize quartz pre-sorbed Cu and Pb only at low NaCl concentrations. The dissolved (<3 kDa) Cu and Pb increases (37–67% of Cu and 31–81% of Pb) with the increase in NaCl concentration. This behavior can be attributed to the reduced sorption on CD and quartz, and increased solubility of Cu with the increased Cl− concentration. The higher dissolved Pb (30–81%) compared to Cu (37–67%) could be attributed to the higher solubility of Pb in chloride rich solutions.

Cu speciation is dominated by precipitation of solid phases (86–74% of Cu formed precipitates) including Malachite (Cu2CO3(OH)2) and Atacamite (Cu2Cl (OH)3) ((Fig. S2a)). Dissolved Cu represents 14–26% of total Cu, and is dominated by Cu2+ (13–25%, Fig. S2c).

Pb precipitates as cerrusite at low Cl−, and the cerrusite concentration decrease with the increase in Cl− concentration. Only 16% of Pb occurs as dissolved species at 1 mM Cl− and the concentration of dissolved Pb species increases linearly to 100% at 165 mM Cl− (Fig. S2b). Dissolved lead is dominated by Pb2+ (12–18%) and PbCl+ (18–57%) below and above 50 mM NaCl, respectively (Fig. S2d).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of experimental conditions on facilitated transport and remobilization of quartz pre-sorbed Cu and Pb
ce:section.ce:para: The high sorption of Cu and Pb on CD suggest that CDs can be promising candidate for efficient binding of Cu and Pb from aqueous solutions. Thus, it is not hard to imagine that CDs have a significant potential for Cu and Pb facilitated transport in porous media. In particular, with the higher sorption of Cu and Pb on CDs than on quartz sand used in this study. Such competitive sorption would ultimately influence the pre-deposited Cu and Pb in sand columns (Kamrani et al., 2017). Here, we discuss the effect of experimental conditions including IS, pH, initial CD concentration, and clay content in sand column on CD, Cu and Pb retention and transport in porous media. Fig. 5 shows SBTCs of CD, Cu and Pb at four different CD concentrations (200–800 mg L−1) and constant IS (1 mM NaCl) and pH conditions (6). Fig. 6 presents the sequential break through curves (SBTCs) of CD, Cu and Pb at different pH (Chen et al., 2017; Chen et al., 2010; Cornell and Schwertmann, 2006) and constant IS (1 mM NaCl) and CD concentration (400 mg L−1). Fig. 7 presents the SBTCs of CD, Pb and Cu at different IS (1–200 mM NaCl) and constant pH (Chen et al., 2017) and CD concentration (400 mg L−1). Fig. 8 presents the SBTCs of CDs, Cu and Pb obtained in columns packed quartz mixed with different clay content (0–30% Kaolin) under constant IS (1 mM NaCl), pH (Chen et al., 2017) and CD concentration (400 mg L−1). In these figures, C/C0 for CD, Cu, Pb and conservative tracer are plotted versus the number of pore volumes. The SBTCs of Cu and Pb at different experimental conditions include two parts: 1) before CD injection and 2) after CD injection and are discussed below. Under all experimental conditions, the transport of Cu and Pb in quartz media exhibited typical breakthrough behaviors after CD injection; the C/C0 quickly increased to a peak value and decreased quickly to zero after the columns were flushed with background solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of ionic strength and pH on cotransport of CDs, Cu and Pb
ce:section.ce:para:
CDs exhibited high mobility at all experimental conditions (C/C0 of 0.98, 0.94 and 1.04: Fig. 4a–c). Similarly, Cu and Pb exhibited higher mobility (C/C0 of 0.98–0.84 and 0.86–0.74 for Cu and Pb, respectively, Fig. 4a–c) compared to those in the absence of CDs (C/C0 < 0.4 for Cu and no BTC was observed for Pb, Figs. 5 and 6
). Evidently, CD-facilitated transport was solely responsible for the observed Cu and Pb enhanced mobility. This is in agreement with the decreased sorption of Cu and Pb on quartz in the presence of CDs (Figs. 2 and 3).

The mobility of CDs was not impacted by the increase in ionic strength or pH (Fig. 4a–c). CD and quartz were negatively charged over the range of solution conditions investigated (Table 3), resulting in unfavorable conditions for CD-CD aggregation and CD-sand attachment. DLVO curves in Fig. S3 further suggested that the calculated energy barriers were higher than 5 k
B
T for all experimental conditions investigated. These energy barriers and the long distance of secondary minimum energy prevented CD-CD aggregation and quartz-CD attachment, resulting in high CD mobility, which together with the high affinity of CD to Cu and Pb, present the necessary prerequisite of the subsequent facilitated transport. This is in agreement with the graphene oxide facilitated transport of Cu (Zhou et al., 2016).

The mobility of Cu and Pb decreased with the increase in ionic strength (C/C0 = 0.98 to 0.94: Fig. 4a and b). Similarly, effluent Cu and Pb decreased by approximately 5.8 and 10%, respectively with the increase in ionic strength from 1 to 100 mM NaCl (Fig. 4D). This behavior can be attributed to the decreased sorption of Cu and Pb on CDs with the increase in ionic strength (Fig. 3), resulting in decreased Cu and Pb mobility and decreased Cu and Pb effluent concentrations. Effluent Cu and Pb decreased by approximately 13 and 14% respectively with the increase in pH from 6 to 8 (Fig. 4D). This is likely due to Cu and Pb precipitation with the increase in pH (Fig. S1a).

Copper occur both in dissolved form (~12%) and in solid form (malachite: Cu2CO3(OH)2, 86.5%) at pH 6, 100% as malachite at pH 7 and as tenorite (CuO) at pH 8 (Fig. S1a). Thus, the removal of copper ions can be attributed to the precipitation of malachite and tenorite. The point of zero charge for malachite and tenorite is approximately 8 (Li et al., 2017). Similarly, the point of zero charge for iron oxides on the surface of quartz is approximately 8–9 (Cornell and Schwertmann, 2006). This may result in increased retention of copper with the increase in pH in phases 1–3 (Fig. 6a–c). Additionally, precipitation of malachite and tenorite may result in the formation of large crystals that may be retained by straining in the quartz column.

A small fraction of Pb occurs as dissolved at (~11%) pH 6 and in solid form as cerrusite (~85%). At pH 7, Pb occurs mainly in solid phase as cerrusite (PbCO3 ~ 99%) and at pH 8 as hydrocerrusite (Pb3(CO3)2(OH)2–99.6%) (Fig. S1b). This explains the retention of Pb in the quartz column at all pHs (Fig. 6a–c) either by lead precipitate-quartz interactions, or by straining of lead precipitates.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of CD concentration on the sequential transport of CD, Cu and Pb
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 5 shows the SBTCs of CD, Pb, and Cu at different CD concentrations. In the first three phases, Cu was significantly retained (C/C0 = 0.4) in the quartz column, and Pb was totally retained with no BTC. The introduction of CDs resulted in the remobilization of the retained Cu and Pb indicated by the BTC of Cu and Pb in phases 4 and 5 at all CD concentrations. The normalized Cu and Pb effluent concentrations increased (C/C0 max: 0.32 to 0.82 for Cu and 0.70 to 1.26 for Pb) with the increase in CD concentration from 200 to 400 mg L−1 (Fig. 5a, b) and then decreased with further increase in CD concentration to 800 mg L−1 (Fig. 5c, d). Similarly, the effluent % Cu and Pb mass increased with the increase in CD concentration from 200 to 400 mg L−1 and decreased again with further increase in CD concentration to 800 mg L−1 (Fig. 5e). This suggests that the remobilization of Cu and Pb increased with the increase in CD concentration from 200 to 400 mg L−1 and then decreased with the increase in CD concentration to 800 mg L−1. The initial increase in Cu and Pb remobilization with the increase in CD concentration can be attributed to increased sorption of Cu and Pb with the increase in CD concentration from 200 to 400 mg L−1 (Fig. 1) due to the higher CD surfaces available for metal sorption. The decrease in Cu and Pb remobilization at CD concentrations > 400 mg L−1 could be attributed to the aggregation of CDs at high CD concentrations (He et al., 2008; Baalousha, 2009), which results in increased CD retention in the column by attachment and/or straining mechanisms. CD deposition was concentration-dependent and the % CD mass retained in the columns increased with increased initial CD concentration (4.5 and 9.1% at 200 to 800 mg CD, respectively, Table S2). This behavior can be attributed to the increased straining rate due to the increased CD aggregate size with the increase in CD concentration (Kamrani et al., 2017). Thus, 400 mg L−1 of CDs is the optimum concentration for Pb and Cu remobilization in the sand columns and this concentration was used to investigate the effect of ionic strength and pH on the retention and remobilization of Cu and Pb in quartz columns.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH on the sequential transport of CD, Cu and Pb
ce:section.ce:para: SBTCs obtained at different pH conditions (6, 7 and 8) are presented in Fig. 6. Pb and Cu was retained in the quartz column at all pHs. Pb was fully retained on quartz at all pH conditions, whereas Cu was partially retained at pH 6 and 7 and totally retained at pH 8 (Fig. 6). The mobility of CDs in the quartz remained the same at all pHs (Fig. 6a–c), indicating that CD mobility does not have any role in the decrease in Cu and Pb remobilization with the change in pH. Similarly, Cu and Pb sorption was not affected by the change in pH (Fig. 2b), indicating that Cu and Pb sorption on CD does not play any role in the decrease in Cu and Pb remobilization with the change in pH. Thus, the increased retention of Cu and Pb with the increase in pH can be attributed to the change in Cu and Pb speciation as a function of pH (see discussion above and Fig. S1).

The introduction of CDs to the sand column resulted in remobilization of Cu and Pb in phases 4 and 5 (Fig. 6a–c) due to higher affinity of Cu and Pb to CDs than to quartz (Fig. 2). The remobilization of Cu and Pb was highest at pH 6 (Fig. 6a) and decreased with the increase in pH (Fig. 6b and c). C/C0 max for Pb and Cu decreased from 1.26, 0.82 at pH 6 to 0.4, 0.72 at pH 8 respectively. The lower Cu and Pb remobilization at higher pH could be attributed to the lower solubility of the Cu and Pb solid phases at higher pHs (Reddy et al., 1995).

The lower Pb remobilization compared to Cu at higher pHs () can be due to the lower dissolution rate of cerrusite and hydrocerrusite (K
sp of lead carbonate is ≈7.4 × 10−14 at 25 °C) compared to the solubility of tenorite (K
sp of tenorite is ≈3.5 × 10−9 at 25 °C) (Lide, 2004).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of ionic strength on the sequential transport of CD, Cu and Pb
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 7 shows that in the first three phases (background electrolyte injection, Cu and Pb injection, and second background electrolyte injection), Pb was nearly completely adsorbed by the quartz grains and thus retained in the column. Only a small breakthrough was observed at different ionic strengths. In contrast, Cu showed higher mobility in the quartz column; C/C0 max was approximately 0.4 at 1 mM NaCl which decreased to 0.33 at 200 mM NaCl. These results are expected because of the significant sorption of Cu and Pb on quartz (Fig. 3). The higher retention of Pb can attribute to higher affinity of quartz to Pb than to Cu in the absence of CD (Fig. 3). Additionally, retention of Cu and Pb can be attributed to the precipitation of Cu and Pb (see discussion above and Fig. S2a, b).

Introduction of CDs to the column resulted in release of Pb and Cu (Fig. 7a–c). This can be related to high affinity of Cu and Pb to CDs than to quartz under all experimental conditions (Figs. 1 and 2). Thus, CDs can snatch the pre-adsorbed Cu and Pb from the quartz surface (Fang et al., 2010). The remobilization of Pb was higher than Cu indicated by the higher C/C0 for Pb compared to that for Cu, which can be attributed to the higher sorption of Pb on CDs than Cu (Fig. 2).

The remobilization of Cu and Pb by CDs further confirm that the transport of Cu and Pb through the quartz column is controlled by CD-Cu and CD-Pb binding even at shorter contact time (hydraulic retention time of about 18 min = 1PV time). This provide further evidence for the mechanism driving the facilitated transport of Cu and Pb by CDs. Compared to the traditional cotransport experiment in pre-equilibrated mode, the sequential elution experiment in this study presents a new perspective on interactions between CD and Cu and Pb. The CD-facilitated transport could work even at a short contact time due to the higher affinity of Cu and Pb to CDs than to quartz.

The normalized effluent CD concentration and the effluent CD mass decreased with the increase in ionic strength from 1 to 200 mM NaCl (Fig. 7a–d), which indicated slight increase in CD retention in the quartz column of CDs with the increase in ionic strength. The introduction of CDs resulted in the mobilization of pre-sorbed Cu and Pb. The remobilization of Cu and Pb decreased with the increase in ionic strength, which can attribute to the decrease in the Cu and Pb sorption on CDs with the increase in ionic strength (Fig. 3), and the slight increase in CD retention in the quartz column with the increase in ionic strength (Ranđelović et al., 2011). CD were more efficient in remobilizing Pb compared with Cu, in good agreement with the higher sorption of Pb (Fig. 2b) on CDs compared with Cu (Fig. 2a).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of clay content on the sequential transport of CD, Cu and Pb
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of the presence of kaolinite (kaolin) in the sand columns on CD, Cu and Pb transports were investigated by comparing the transport in sand columns amended with 10, 20 and 30 wt% kaolin. Kaolin amendment resulted in full retention of Cu and Pb in the quartz column in the first three phases (Fig. 8 ).

CDs showed fairly high retention (similar to tracer) in phases 4 and 5, and the retention of CD increased with the increase in kaolin content in the quartz column (Fig. 8b–d). Small Cu and Pb BTC curve was observed at 10% kaolin amendment, which decreased with the increase in % kaolin content in the quartz column. % Cu and Pb detected in the effluent was respectively 13.1% and 3.4% for 10 wt% of kaolin, and decreased with the increase in kaolin content in the quartz columns (Fig. 8e). These results can be attributed to the increased retention of CD in the columns with the increased kaolin content and the high affinity of kaolin to Cu and Pb (Shahmohammadi et al., 2011).

CD facilitated the transport of Cu and Pb in quartz sand columns and remobilized pre-sorbed Cu and Pb from quartz columns under all experimental conditions investigated in this study except in the presence of kaolinite in the column. CD exhibited high mobility (>87% elution of CDs) in the quartz columns under all experimental conditions except in the presence of kaolinite. Complexation of Cu and Pb with CDs is responsible for the facilitated transport and remobilization of Cu and Pb. Highest remobilization of pre-sorbed Cu and Pb was achieved at 400 mg L−1 CD. Remobilization of pre-sorbed Cu and Pb decreased with the increase in pH, NaCl concentration, and clay content. In the sequential elution experiment, the CD-facilitated remobilization of Cu and Pb occurred in a quiet short contact time (ca. 18 min). Thus, CD with high mobility and high affinity to Cu and Pb could serve as an effective carrier for Cu and Pb at all experimental conditions investigated in this study.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.scitotenv.2018.01.184.
